videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here in Queenstown, New Zealand. And in this video, we're going to show you our top things to do here, including a mix of free activities and can't miss highlights. So hope you enjoy it! Queenstown is located on the shores of Lake Wakatipu on New Zealand's South Island. This vibrant alpine town is famed for its dramatic landscapes, where crystal-clear waters meet towering mountains. Queenstown is also known as the adventure capital of the world, offering a wealth of outdoor activities. But beyond the adrenaline rush, a relaxed pace of life also permeates through the town. In this video, we highlight 14 of the best things to do in and around Queenstown, including a range of free activities as well as paid ones. You can find links to current pricing for all attractions in the description below the video. Make sure to watch until the end as we'll also share a few important travel tips. Now let's dive in! We are Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures! Queenstown center is a true hub of activity. You should definitely take a stroll along the waterfront, where scenic cafés, restaurants, and street food stalls line the shores of Lake Wakatipu. There's also a small beach at the lakeshore, which is a popular swimming spot when it's warm and sunny. In and around Beech Street and Shotover Street, you can browse a range of high-end boutiques and outdoor stores. The legendary Fergburger restaurant can also be found in this area, and this is a Queenstown icon. There's always a long line here no matter what time of the day. As the evening approaches, the town center really comes alive with a mix of bars and diverse restaurants. Located on a small peninsula next to the waterfront, Queenstown Gardens is a nice place to combine with your visit to the town center. These gardens boast lush green spaces as well as stunning alpine vistas. And as a bonus, they're free to enter. Stroll along the lake, take in the colorful flowers, or enjoy recreational activities like lawn bowling, tennis, and frisbee golf. Moke Lake is just a short 15-minute drive from the bustling center of Queenstown and offers a serene escape. You'll find calm reflections on the tranquil waters here. The lake is perfect for a relaxing getaway and offers fishing opportunities as well as walking trails. Reward yourself with breathtaking views on the easy Moke Lake Loop walk, which is good for all fitness levels. The lake's edge is also a popular camping spot, with numerous lakeshore pitches that are ideal for an overnight stay under starry skies. A scenic 40-minute drive from Queenstown center brings you to the Remarkables, a mountain range famed for its dramatic peaks. In the winter, this place is a bustling ski resort while summer makes for nice hiking. The Lake Alta Track is a popular choice, offering a short and relatively easy hike to a stunning alpine lake. The trail starts at the parking lot of the Remarkable Ski Area and involves a steady uphill climb. This includes a mix of gravel paths and some rocky sections. Once you reach the lake, you'll be treated to amazing views of the glacial water and surrounding peaks. Queenstown is renowned for its breathtaking sunsets, boasting numerous spots to enjoy the evening glow. Our favorite sunset spot was Coronet Peak, a mere 20-minute drive from Queenstown center. This popular ski area becomes a peaceful retreat in the summer, free from the usual crowds. From the large parking lot at the base of the chairlifts, you can watch the sun bathe the surrounding valley in a soft golden light. If you're looking for another sunset area that's within walking distance of the town center, then Queenstown Lakefront is a great option. And Lake Hayes is a nice alternative option for peace and quiet. Embark on a picturesque journey across Lake Wakatipu aboard the historic TSS Earnslaw, which departs from Queenstown's waterfront. Built in 1912, this coal-fired steam ship once served as a vital transport link carrying passengers, livestock, and goods to remote lakeside communities. Today, it stands as a unique tourist attraction. Enjoy a relaxing cruise while soaking in the stunning scenery and charming rustic atmosphere of the ship. We also opted for a tour of Walter Peak High Country Farm as part of our TSS Earnslaw experience, where we first savored a delightful afternoon tea with freshly baked biscuits. Our farm host then guided us through feeding Highland cows, alpacas, and other animals. The highlight was the farm show, demonstrating the interesting process of sheep shearing. At the end of the tour, we had some free time to stroll around the gardens before returning to the boat. This whole experience took about 4 hours, and you can find a link to the same tour we did in the description below the video. Queenstown serves as a gateway to many incredible day trips, but Glenorchy stands out as our favorite. This charming hamlet, situated at the northern tip of Lake Wakatipu, is a scenic 45-minute drive along the lake's eastern shore. The journey to get there is a highlight in itself, with winding roads revealing everchanging landscapes. The town of Glenorchy offers jetboating and canoeing on the Dart River, serene waterfront strolls, and a rustic downtown area. Grab a coffee or some lunch in one of the local restaurants to soak in the atmosphere. Another 30 minutes past the town you can also find the trailhead for the famous Routeburn Track. If you're looking to fit in a section of this hike on the same day as exploring the town, you'll most likely only be able to go to Routeburn Falls Hut and back. Other popular day trip options from Queenstown include the Gibbston Wine Valley, Wānaka, or Milford Sound. Just a 20-minute drive from Queenstown center, Bob's Cove is the perfect spot to relax and soak up the sun. This protected bay is nestled along the edge of Lake Wakatipu and provides calm and shallow waters that are ideal for swimming. Beyond the beach, a short walk leads to a breathtaking viewpoint overlooking the cove and surrounding landscape. Just be aware that the parking lot is very small and requires a short walk to reach the cove. The Queenstown Hill hike begins from Belfast Street, which is a short distance from the town center. This out-and-back trail is about 4.8 km or 3 miles total. With 400 meters or 1,312 feet of elevation gain, we would rate the hike as moderate difficulty. The initial climb is steep, but the path soon levels out to a gradual slope, winding through shaded areas with tall trees. Since the trail is very well-maintained, this is a hike for everyone. From the summit, you'll be rewarded with breathtaking vistas of Queenstown as well as the Remarkables and Lake Wakatipu. It's also possible to start this hike from the town center, but be prepared for an additional 100 meters or 328 feet of elevation gain, making the ascent slightly more challenging. As the "Adventure Capital of the World", Queenstown offers a range of thrilling experiences. Adrenaline junkies truly have endless options here. Choose from jet boat rides, bungee jumping, paragliding, ziplining, skydiving, and more. Notably the Kawarau Bridge holds historical significance as the world's first commercial bungee jumping site. And even if you don't jump, it's still fun to witness the action from the viewing platforms here. We opted for a jet boat ride during our visit, which is a Queenstown staple. With numerous operators, we chose KJet for a scenic 1-hour adventure. Their ride took us across Lake Wakatipu as well as along the Shotover and Kawarau rivers. The biggest advantage of the KJet company is that their jet boats leave right from the Queenstown waterfront. Our favorite part of the experience was when our captain did his wild 180 spins. Just a 20-minute drive from Queenstown center, Arrowtown offers a charming step back in time. This historic gold mining town witnessed a huge boom during the 1860s Gold Rush. Today, Arrowtown retains its original character with preserved buildings lining the picturesque main street. You can delve into its gold mining heritage at the Lakes District Museum, wander through the poignant Chinese settlement, try gold panning, or simply check out the cute shops and cafés. A fun way to get back to Queenstown from here is by riding along the Arrow River Trail over the Kawarau Bungee Bridge and through the Gibbston Wine Valley. Located just a short walk from Queenstown center, the Skyline Gondola offers a swift ascent to Bob's Peak. Once at the top, you'll find a platform with panoramic vistas of Lake Wakatipu and Queenstown itself. There's also a souvenir shop, bar, and restaurant at the summit where you can refuel. Besides that, many activities can be accessed from here, including mountain bike trails, paragliding, hiking, and luging. The gondola operates year-round and is generally open from 9 AM until late, with extended hours during peak seasons and holidays. Accessible via the Skyline Gondola, the Queenstown Luge is a family-friendly adventure that you can't miss when you're up there. Start by purchasing a package, which usually includes three or more rides. Then, hop on the scenic chairlift to the luge start point. After a short explanation from the crew about how to ride, you can jet off at your own pace. Two distinct tracks wind their way through banked corners, tunnels, and dippers. You can control your speed with a simple handlebar system, making it easy for riders of all ages and skill levels to enjoy. I'm catching you. Hiking the Ben Lomond Track was our favorite thing to do in Queenstown. This challenging yet rewarding hike is best accessed via the Skyline Gondola, saving you a significant climb. While the Tiki Trail offers a route from town, it adds substantial elevation gain. From the gondola station, the out-and-back hike is 10.8 km or 6.7 miles long, with over 1,000 meters or 3,300 feet of elevation gain. The trail itself offers very little shade, so be prepared for sun exposure. It begins with a gradual ascent to the saddle, which is a nice resting spot to take in views of the surrounding mountains. So we made it to the saddle now, and it took us about an hour and a half to get here. It's already really nice views of the mountains behind us. Now we have about an hour to go up to the summit up there. Once you reach the summit, you'll be rewarded with the best 360-vistas of the area. Be sure to take enough water and sunscreen since it takes about 5 to 6 hours total if you do it from the top of the gondola and back. As we promised at the beginning of the video, we'll now share a few travel tips for Queenstown. Queenstown offers a wealth of attractions, many of which are located outside the town center. Having a car provides the freedom to explore at your own pace. It also allows you to visit nearby gems like Arrowtown or Glenorchy and access trailheads for stunning hikes. While the Queenstown center is small and easily explored on foot, other destinations around it are not. While a day trip to Milford Sound is a popular thing to do from Queenstown, it's a really long journey with limited time to experience the region's full splendor. Consider dedicating a few days to exploring Fiordland National Park and stay overnight in Te Anau or Milford Sound itself. The same is true for Wānaka. Finally, Queenstown center is a good area to stay with a wide range of accommodation options. We stayed at a campground at the edge of town, within walking distance of the main attractions in the center. Depending on your activity choices, you could also consider staying beyond central Queenstown. Areas like Lake Hayes Estate or Frankton offer alternative options. Check the description below the video for various accommodation options. If you have more time in Queenstown or looking for other things to do, we put a list of different options on the screen here for you. We also included other important links in the description that will help with your Queenstown experience, such as an interactive map so you can get to each place we mentioned. Queenstown, New Zealand is known as the adventure capital of the world, and for good reason! Nestled in the heart of the Southern Alps on the shores of Lake Wakatipu, Queenstown offers visitors breathtaking scenery and a wide range of thrilling activities. In this video, we'll be sharing some of the best things to do in Queenstown, New Zealand.

First on our list is bungee jumping. Queenstown is the birthplace of commercial bungee jumping, and you can experience this adrenaline-pumping activity at the Kawarau Bridge, which is the world's first commercial bungee site. You can also try the Nevis Bungy, which is the highest bungee jump in New Zealand, or the Ledge Bungy, which allows you to jump from a platform suspended 400 meters above Queenstown.

If you're a fan of winter sports, Queenstown is a must-visit destination. The city has four major ski fields, including Coronet Peak, which is only a 20-minute drive from the city center. You can also try your hand at snowboarding, snowshoeing, or even ice climbing.

For a more laid-back activity, take a scenic cruise on Lake Wakatipu. You can relax on board and take in the stunning scenery, or get off at one of the stops along the way and explore the local area. You can also take a ride on the iconic TSS Earnslaw steamship, which has been cruising on the lake for over 100 years.

For a bird's eye view of Queenstown, take a gondola ride up to the Skyline Queenstown complex. From here, you can enjoy panoramic views of the city and the surrounding mountains. You can also take a thrilling ride on the Skyline Luge, which is a gravity-driven karting experience.

If you're a Lord of the Rings fan, don't miss the chance to visit some of the film's iconic locations. You can take a tour of the filming locations, including the Misty Mountains and the Pillars of the Kings, or visit the Weta Workshop, which created many of the film's props and costumes.

Last but not least, foodies will love the vibrant dining scene in Queenstown. The city has a wide range of restaurants and cafes, serving everything from traditional New Zealand cuisine to international dishes. Be sure to try some of the local specialties, such as venison, lamb, and seafood.

In conclusion, Queenstown is a city that offers a unique blend of natural beauty, adventure, and culture.

You're standing on the edge of a 43-meter bridge above Lake Wakatipu, about to take the leap that'll define your entire New Zealand adventure. That's Queenstown for you. With only 3 days in New Zealand's adventure capital, most travelers make costly mistakes that leave them exhausted or missing the best experiences. This guide reveals exactly how to experience world-class adventure, hidden local gems, and Instagram-worthy moments without tourist traps. We're covering three perfectly planned days, insider booking strategies, and the one mistake that costs travelers $400. Let's dive in. 

DAY 1: GET YOUR BEARINGS 
Day one is all about orientation and easing into Queenstown's energy. Start the morning at 8 AM with the Skyline Gondola - and here's why this timing matters. Most tourists sleep in and hit the gondola around 10 AM when it's packed. Beat the crowds, get better photos, and you'll pay around $60 NZD for adults instead of fighting for space later. At the top, skip the overpriced restaurant and do two luge rides instead - one isn't enough. The views of The Remarkables mountain range from up here are what postcards are made of, and you'll understand why Queenstown's geography makes it perfect for adventure sports. The gondola terminal offers multiple viewing platforms, each providing different perspectives of the dramatic alpine landscape that surrounds this adventure capital. After descending, spend late morning exploring Queenstown Gardens. This isn't just a filler activity - the lakefront walk gives you perspective on distances and helps you plan the rest of your stay. Plus, it's free and offers some of the best photo opportunities with Lake Wakatipu as your backdrop. The gardens feature mature trees, open lawns perfect for picnics, and waterfront paths that showcase Queenstown's natural setting between mountains and lake. For lunch, head to Steamer Wharf area where you'll find several waterfront restaurants. Here's the local secret - many restaurants like Boardwalk or Finz have more casual takeaway options for approximately $35-40 NZD instead of their full dining service at around $60-70. Same quality, better views eating by the lake. Your afternoon centerpiece is the TSS Earnslaw steamship cruise to Walter Peak Farm. Book online in advance for the best rates - expect to pay roughly $75-85 NZD for the cruise only. This 90-minute experience gives you lake perspectives impossible to get from land, and the vintage steamship is genuinely historic, not a tourist reproduction. The coal-fired steam engine operates exactly as it did when launched in 1912. But before you book that expensive helicopter tour everyone talks about, save your money. The steamship views are spectacular, and you'll want that helicopter budget for day two's adventures. The Walter Peak experience includes sheep shearing demonstrations and farm tours that provide insight into New Zealand's agricultural heritage. Evening is for exploring Queenstown's compact city center. Dinner at Rātā restaurant if you're splurging - Chef Josh Emett's New Zealand cuisine is worth approximately $95 per person. Budget option? Head to Devil Burger for about $32 NZD - locals consistently rate it above the tourist-trap restaurants on the main strip. End day one with drinks at Atlas Beer Café. Local craft beers, reasonable prices, and you'll overhear conversations about hidden gems for the next two days. 

DAY 2: ADVENTURE DAY 
Day two is where Queenstown earns its adventure capital reputation. Start early - 9 AM booking for Shotover Jet. Here's the insider strategy: book directly through their website three days in advance and pay $169 NZD for adults. The famous 360-degree spins in narrow canyon walls deliver exactly the adrenaline rush you came for. Now here's something nobody tells you about the timing. Most adventure companies offer morning and afternoon slots, but morning activities pair better with afternoon scenic experiences. Your body processes adrenaline better when you're fresh, and afternoon light is superior for photography on scenic activities. This strategic timing also means you'll have energy for multiple activities without the fatigue that builds throughout the day. If bungee jumping is on your list, AJ Hackett Kawarau Gorge is the original commercial bungee site.

Cell phone coverage works throughout Queenstown and most scenic routes, [music] but Paradise Valley has dead zones. Download offline maps before heading to Glenori. Most activities include free Wi-Fi for sharing those Instagram worthy shots immediately. International visitors should consider purchasing a local SIM card for better rates and coverage throughout New Zealand. Mistakes to avoid. Here are the expensive mistakes that cost travelers hundreds of dollars. First, booking activities through hotel concierages. They add 15 to 20% commission that goes directly into hotel profits. Book directly with activity companies or through their official websites. This applies especially to popular activities like helicopter tours, jet boating, and bungee jumping where the markup can be significant. Second mistake, not having backup plans for weather. Queenstown weather changes hourly. When helicopter tours get cancelled, travelers without alternatives waste entire afternoons. Always have indoor backup activities planned like the Kiwi Bird [music] Life Park, wine tasting, or Queenstown's underwater observatory at the Maintown Pier. Weather dependent activities are particularly affected during winter months and shoulder seasons. Third, eating every meal on the main tourist [music] strip. Those restaurants survive on location, not quality. The best food hides on side streets [music] or requires short drives. Tourist strip restaurants charge premium prices for mediocre food, often targeting visitors who won't return to complain about poor value. [music] Fourth mistake, underestimating travel times between activities. Queenstown may look compact, but mountain roads take longer than expected. Allow extra [music] time for scenic stops and potential weather delays, especially when traveling to Milford Sound or other remote locations. Finally, trying to pack too much into each day. [music] Queenstown's adventure activities are physically demanding. Book one major activity per day, not three. You'll enjoy experiences more and avoid the exhaustion that ruins day three for many visitors. Adventure fatigue is real, and recovery time between activities enhances rather than diminishes your overall experience. 3 days that'll give you both the Instagram shots and the authentic Queenstown experience [music] most tourists miss. Which day appeals to you most? If this guide saved you hours of research and potentially hundreds of dollars in tourist traps, give this video a thumbs up and subscribe to Explorers Radar for more destination guides that go beyond the obvious. Check out our next destination guide where we explore another incredible location with the same insider approach. Thanks for watching Explorers Radar and we'll see you in the next adventure.

videos.transcript: Come with us while we try (and rank) Queenstown's 10 best foodie spots. Queenstown is blessed with not only unparalleled natural beauty but also an incredible and diverse food scene. Before heading off on our trip we did some intense research on the top rated places to eat. To help you maximize your experience and avoid any disappointing meals, we tried 10 of the best foodie spots and ranked them from our time there. There are certainly no losers on this list however starting at number 10 is the world-renowned Ferg Burger which is a New Zealand institution and dubbed best burger on the planet by CNN. So of all the places in New Zealand and Queenstown that we've wanted to try it's definitely Ferg Burger, the line there was ridiculous it took about 40 minutes to get our burger. So I got what was known as The Ferg Deluxe with bacon and the beef patty. Let's see. Delicious so far. So it's more like a, not like a fast food burger, it's more like a, definitely like a home style cooked burger. Like genuine beef patty, chutney, aoli, bacon, lettuce, onion. Good. I like it. So I've almost finished my burger and now I can give a final verdict and it was an enjoyable burger. It was nice. But do I think I'd wait 40 minutes for it again? Probably not. So it was an institution, I'm glad I've tried it. I enjoyed the burger, but didn't sweep me off my feet. Coming in at number nine is Bespoke Kitchen. This trendy breakfast spot is incredibly popular in Queenstown. So we're continuing our hunt for the best food in Queenstown and we're at the Bespoke Kitchen which is meant to be the place to go for brunch, and the food looks insane. I've got an eggs benedict and Pete has avocado toast. My eggs benedict was done perfectly and we really enjoyed their delicious strong coffee. If you don't have time to take a wine tour, The Winery in Queenstown is a must visit. Number eight on our list is a really unique offering. Here you can try more than 60 wines by a taste, a half glass or a full glass. This was really fun and a great way to discover New Zealand's wines. So we've decided to try the most expensive wine here. The tasting was $18. It smells like chocolate and cherries. I really like that. Super complex. Coming in at number seven was another of the Ferg family, Ferg Bakery. Maybe we had lower expectations than we had for the burger which helped but our quick takeaway breakfast here was fantastic. This ham and cheese croissant is delicious. It's got leg ham in there, and swiss cheese, and then we've just got their standard famous Ferg pie, so we'll let you know how it is. Super thick ham, super tasty. So croissants are Grace's world, but pies are mine so I'll be the first one to taste this one and see how it is. That meat inside is so tender. How's the pepper sauce? Super flavoursome. I wasn't sure what it was going to be like but it's super delicious. Number six is Sherwood, which is about a 10-minute drive outside of the center of town, but such a great place for food and delicious cocktails and in a really cool setting. All our dishes here hit the spot but the hero dish was without a doubt the lamb bacon, onion and pecorino cheese wood fired pizza. Number five, Margos, is a funky fresh Mexican spot that plays with traditional flavours to put an interesting twist on cult favourite classics. The starters are where this restaurant really impresses. The queso fundido was incredibly addictive and the Coca-Cola barbecue pork ribs were mind-blowingly tender. Perky's, which is number four on our list, is not a place for food but instead it's a unique and welcoming venue for some tasty cocktails and soaking in the panoramic views of Lake Wakatipu and the impressive surrounding mountain peaks. This floating boat bar was so much fun and if you are hungry they're more than happy to let you bring in takeaway food from the surrounding restaurants. One of the best breakfast I've ever had was at restaurant number three, Odd Saint. My breakfast burger was like my favourite sausage and egg McMuffin but a million times better, if that's even possible. The rosti upgrade was definitely worth it. This was by far our favourite cafe in Queenstown. The silver medal goes to Botswana Butchery. We wanted somewhere special for Grace's birthday dinner and this was the perfect choice. We'd been looking forward to this meal for weeks and we weren't disappointed. The service, wine and most importantly the food was flawless. We rolled out of the restaurant after over ordering on oysters, a tender cut of steak and too many sides, but definitely had no regrets. With three Good Food hats it's no surprise that Amisfield comes in at number one on our list. Located in Central Otago, so technically outside of Queenstown, this winery is a culinary institution. The menu explores the season's best local produce in an incredibly innovative and thoughtful way. The location of the restaurant on the winery is just as beautiful and you can even start your day with a tasting of the winery's award-winning wines. Every time we've told people that we are going to Queenstown everyone kept saying you have to go to Amisfield winery for a meal. We're really excited it looks super interesting and artistic and hot tip we booked for lunch it's about half the price, less courses but it'll be plenty for us I think. Yeah we're not a big fan of like the 12 course degustations anyway. So a few less courses probably suits us better, but it was much cheaper option than doing the dinner. We're really excited so we'll see you there. How is it? Far more delicious than I thought it was going to be when it was like asparagus five ways, and then drink. Every single spot that we visited in Queenstown was incredible. You can see why this city is world famous for its food. The emphasis on fresh produce combined with an eclectic mix of cultures creates an amazing food culture. A small city with more than 150 bars, restaurants and cafes, we really only scratched the surface of the Queenstown food scene. We will definitely be back to taste more of what Queenstown has to offer. Thank you for watching. If you enjoyed this video, please help us reach a wider audience by hitting that like button and clicking subscribe.
